labels.name: Interface
labels.desc: A contract specifying what a class must be able to do

labels.name: Implements
labels.desc: A class fulfilling an interface contract

labels.name: Generic
labels.desc: A type placeholder that makes code work with any specified type

labels.name: Stream
labels.desc: A declarative pipeline of operations applied to a collection

labels.name: Lambda
labels.desc: An inline, anonymous function passed as a value

labels.name: Filter
labels.desc: Keeping only the elements that satisfy a condition

labels.name: Transform
labels.desc: Converting each element into a new form

labels.name: Collect
labels.desc: Gathering the results of a pipeline back into a usable structure

prompts.title: Bounded Generic Collection
prompts.statement: An application needs a reusable container that holds any type of item but enforces a maximum capacity set when the container is created. Adding an item beyond the limit should be rejected.

expectedTags.phrase: holds any type of item
expectedTags.explanation: A container that works with any type of item requires a type placeholder — the classic use case for a generic class that is defined once but works across different data types.

expectedTags.phrase: enforces a maximum capacity set when the container is created
expectedTags.explanation: The capacity constraint is baked into the generic class at construction time, demonstrating how a generic type can carry its own configuration alongside the type parameter.

expectedTags.phrase: Adding an item beyond the limit should be rejected
expectedTags.explanation: Rejecting items that exceed the limit is a filtering decision — only items that satisfy the 'not full' condition are allowed through; the rest are turned away.

prompts.title: Generic Last Element
prompts.statement: A utility method needs to return the last item in any list, regardless of what type the list holds. If the list is empty, it should signal that nothing was found rather than throwing an exception.

expectedTags.phrase: return the last item in any list, regardless of what type the list holds
expectedTags.explanation: A method that works with any list type — whether a list of names, numbers, or orders — requires a generic type parameter so the same code handles all of them without duplication.

expectedTags.phrase: If the list is empty, it should signal that nothing was found
expectedTags.explanation: Checking whether the list is empty before proceeding is a filtering guard — it ensures the method only continues when there is actually an element to return, and signals absence otherwise.

prompts.title: Comparable Records Contract
prompts.statement: The application has both students and products that need to be sortable. Rather than building sorting logic into each class separately, define a shared contract that both classes must fulfill so that sorting can work the same way for either. Each class provides its own rank value to satisfy the contract.

expectedTags.phrase: define a shared contract that both classes must fulfill
expectedTags.explanation: A shared contract is the definition of an interface — it specifies what any participating class must be able to do without dictating how each class does it.

expectedTags.phrase: Each class provides its own rank value to satisfy the contract
expectedTags.explanation: Each class supplying its own implementation of the contract method is exactly what 'implements' means — the class fulfills the interface's promise with its own specific logic.

expectedTags.phrase: sorting can work the same way for either
expectedTags.explanation: Because both classes honor the same contract, a single inline sorting rule can operate on either type — the interface enables the uniformity and the lambda expresses the rule inline.

prompts.title: Tenured Employee Names
prompts.statement: An HR report needs a sorted list of names for every employee who has been with the company for more than five years. The result should contain only the names, in alphabetical order.

expectedTags.phrase: every employee who has been with the company for more than five years
expectedTags.explanation: Narrowing the employee collection down to those exceeding a tenure threshold is a filter operation; the condition 'more than five years' is expressed as an inline rule — a lambda — applied to each employee.

expectedTags.phrase: only the names
expectedTags.explanation: Reducing each full employee record down to just a name converts one form of data into another — the defining characteristic of a transform (map) operation.

expectedTags.phrase: a sorted list of names
expectedTags.explanation: Sorting and then assembling the results into a list are the final two stages of a stream pipeline — the stream enables chaining the sort, and collect gathers the output into a usable list structure.

prompts.title: High-Value Transaction Total
prompts.statement: An accounting report needs the combined dollar amount of all completed transactions over $500. Transactions that are pending, cancelled, or under the threshold should not be included.

expectedTags.phrase: all completed transactions over $500
expectedTags.explanation: Two conditions must both be satisfied — status must be 'completed' and amount must exceed $500 — making this a two-stage filter, each condition written as an inline lambda rule.

expectedTags.phrase: Transactions that are pending, cancelled, or under the threshold should not be included
expectedTags.explanation: Explicitly excluding records that fail one or more conditions is the essence of filtering — only the elements that pass every check survive to the next stage of the pipeline.

expectedTags.phrase: combined dollar amount
expectedTags.explanation: Summing individual transaction amounts into one total is a terminal reduction at the end of a stream pipeline — the stream processes each element and the sum operation assembles the final result.

prompts.title: Long Words Across Sentences
prompts.statement: A text analysis feature needs every unique word longer than four characters found across a list of sentences, regardless of which sentence it came from. The result should have no duplicates.

expectedTags.phrase: found across a list of sentences
expectedTags.explanation: Processing items across multiple sentences requires a stream pipeline that flattens each sentence into its individual words — a transform step that changes the shape of the data from sentences to words.

expectedTags.phrase: every unique word longer than four characters
expectedTags.explanation: Keeping only words that exceed four characters is a filter condition applied to each word in the flattened stream — words that are too short are discarded.

expectedTags.phrase: The result should have no duplicates
expectedTags.explanation: Eliminating duplicate words is a filtering-style step that keeps only first occurrences; gathering the surviving unique words into a final list is a collect operation that terminates the pipeline.

prompts.title: Generic Filter Method
prompts.statement: Multiple parts of the application need to narrow down lists, but the type of item in the list varies. Write a method that accepts any list and a rule, and returns a new list with only the items that satisfy the rule.

expectedTags.phrase: the type of item in the list varies
expectedTags.explanation: When the same method must work whether the list holds names, numbers, or orders, a generic type parameter is required — it acts as a placeholder that is filled in by the caller.

expectedTags.phrase: accepts any list and a rule
expectedTags.explanation: Accepting any list ties back to the generic type parameter, while accepting a rule means the caller passes an inline function — a lambda — that describes which items should be kept.

expectedTags.phrase: returns a new list with only the items that satisfy the rule
expectedTags.explanation: Keeping only items that pass the rule is the filter step; assembling those survivors back into a new list is the collect step — together they form the core of this method's purpose.

prompts.title: Customer Order Totals
prompts.statement: A sales dashboard needs to group all orders by customer and show the total value of each customer's purchases. The output should be keyed by customer name.

expectedTags.phrase: group all orders by customer
expectedTags.explanation: Grouping items from a collection requires streaming through every order and collecting them into a map structure — the stream provides the pipeline and collect with a grouping accumulator builds the grouped result.

expectedTags.phrase: show the total value of each customer's purchases
expectedTags.explanation: Computing a running total per customer transforms the individual order amounts into a single summary figure, and that summary is collected into the final map value for each customer key.

expectedTags.phrase: keyed by customer name
expectedTags.explanation: Organizing the results so each customer name maps to their total is a collect operation that produces a Map — the key choice (customer name) is specified as part of the grouping collector.
